From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

China's video game industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/China_video_game_market_per_platform_2015.png/330px-China_video_game_market_per_platform_2015.png)](/wiki/File:China_video_game_market_per_platform_2015.png)

Games market of China by revenue per platform in 2015[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The video game industry in [China](/wiki/China "China") currently is one of the major markets for the global [video game industry](/wiki/Video_game_industry "Video game industry"), where more than half a billion people play video games. Revenues from [China](/wiki/China "China") make up around 25% of the nearly US$100 billion global video game industry as of 2018. Since 2015, China has exceeded the contribution to the global market from the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Because of its market size, China has been described as the "Games Industry Capital of the World" and is home to some of the largest video game companies.[[3]](#cite_note-3) China has also been a major factor in the growth of [esports](/wiki/Esports "Esports"), both in player talent and in revenue.

China has not always been a major factor in the industry, having been on the verge of economic recovery during the industry's formulative years in the 1970s and 1980s. With the introduction of the [second-generation home gaming consoles](/wiki/Second_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Second generation of video game consoles") in the mid-1980s, a new [black market](/wiki/Black_market "Black market") of illegally-imported goods and [video game clones](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") arose to avoid the high costs of imports, driving away foreign companies. Notably, China imposed a near-complete ban on video game consoles in 2000, fearing the [addiction-like impact of games](/wiki/Video_game_addiction "Video game addiction") on its youths; the ban was ultimately lifted in 2015. During that time, China's video game market greatly expanded in the area of [computer games](/wiki/Computer_game "Computer game") (including [massively multiplayer online games](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_game "Massively multiplayer online game"), [browser games](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game"), [social network games](/wiki/Social_network_game "Social network game"), etc.) and later [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_games "Mobile games"), all which could be [free to play](/wiki/Free_to_play "Free to play") titles with [monetization](/wiki/Video_game_monetization "Video game monetization") to appeal to the average lower income of Chinese players. This massive growth from 2007 to 2013 led the games' publishers and operating companies like [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent") and [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase") to become large global companies. Despite the legitimate growth of the industry, China's video game market continues to be offset by [illegal importing](/wiki/Smuggling "Smuggling"), [copyright violation](/wiki/Copyright_violation "Copyright violation") and [intellectual property theft](/wiki/Intellectual_property_theft "Intellectual property theft").

As with other parts of its media, China's government has strong oversight of the video game industry; all new titles go through a governmental approval process to assure that content aligns with the nation's values. In 2018, an approvals freeze due to the reorganisation of China's content vetting agencies caused numerous game releases to be held up. The video game market plummeted for a year. The government also fears the potential for its youths to become addicted to video games, and have required games to include anti-addiction measures. User verification is used to enforce playtime restrictions, which currently limit minors to three hours per week.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Broadly, the growth of the video game market in China is tied to expansion of its [technology and digital economy](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_China "Science and technology in China") from the 1990s to present day, which by 2016 represented over 30% of its [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product").[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4)

### Initial growth (1980s–2000)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Initial growth (1980s–2000)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Tongyang_-_downtown_-_internet_cafe_-_CIMG9869.JPG/250px-Tongyang_-_downtown_-_internet_cafe_-_CIMG9869.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tongyang_-_downtown_-_internet_cafe_-_CIMG9869.JPG)

An internet café in Tongyang Town, the county seat of Tongshan County, Hubei

At the time that the video game industry was being established in North America in the 1970s, China was [in the midst of major political and economic reform](/wiki/History_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China_(1976%E2%80%931989) "History of the People's Republic of China (1976–1989)") following the death of [Mao Zedong](/wiki/Mao_Zedong "Mao Zedong") in 1976. The country was technologically behind much of the rest of the world in terms of its media. Part of the reform was modernization of its media systems, helping to boost economic prosperity for citizens.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) As such, China saw little of [arcade games](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") or the [first generation of home consoles](/wiki/First_generation_of_video_game_consoles "First generation of video game consoles"), like the [Atari 2600](/wiki/Atari_2600 "Atari 2600") and locally made *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")* consoles.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

By the 1980s, China's economy had significantly improved.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) After the [video game crash of 1983](/wiki/Video_game_crash_of_1983 "Video game crash of 1983") which devastated the North American video game market, [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") became a dominant factor in the global market leading off the [third generation of consoles](/wiki/Third_generation_of_video_game_consoles "Third generation of video game consoles") such as [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo")'s [Famicom](/wiki/Nintendo_Entertainment_System "Nintendo Entertainment System"). The first widely-available video games in 1980s China were arcade games.[[6]](#cite_note-GuoEymanSun-6): 13 The number of arcades in China increased significantly during fall 1985.[[6]](#cite_note-GuoEymanSun-6): 14

Consoles were introduced to China in the late 1980s and were primarily imported from Japan.[[6]](#cite_note-GuoEymanSun-6): 14 However, importing these into China was costly, with a 130% [tariff](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff") on hardware and games along with [value-added taxes](/wiki/Value-added_tax "Value-added tax").[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) Console systems were in high demand, but because of the high costs of importing, only few foreign companies did so. This created the [video game clone](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") grey market in China – manufacturing of reverse-engineered consoles and games at much lower costs than imported system, even if this required dubious or illegal copyright infringement. Outright [copyright theft ("piracy")](/wiki/Copyright_infringement "Copyright infringement") was also rampant in China due to [the country's poor intellectual property controls](/wiki/Intellectual_property_in_China "Intellectual property in China").[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) The sales of cloned console hardware and games outpaced that of legitimate imports, and further drove many foreign companies away since they could not compete with this area, such that by the 1990s, most video game systems in China were manufactured there.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) [Xiaobawang Company](/wiki/Subor "Subor") created the first Chinese-produced console; later consoles came with a keyboard, or were the keyboard, and were intended both for gaming and educational purposes.[[6]](#cite_note-GuoEymanSun-6): 14 By the end of 1993, over 60% of secondary school students in Beijing owned a gaming console.[[6]](#cite_note-GuoEymanSun-6): 14 China also had a small market of legal consoles. Sega released the [Master System](/wiki/Master_System "Master System") in the late 1980s as the first officially distributed console in China ever, and in the 1990s it continued to distribute newer systems. The most popular was [Mega Drive](/wiki/Mega_Drive "Mega Drive"), which like Famicom was cloned by local manufacturers. Sega was one of the most famous arcade producers alongside Namco in the region. Nintendo entered in 1994 with the Game Boy and Super Nintendo through a Hong Kong distributor. There was also a market for smaller producers like UMC with [Gamate](/wiki/Gamate "Gamate") and [Super A'Can](/wiki/Super_A%27Can "Super A'Can").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

As home ownership of computers in urban China increased after 1995, the Chinese PC games market grew significantly.[[6]](#cite_note-GuoEymanSun-6): 14

Console games continued to grow in popularity through the 1990s, which created a broader concern in the media of video game addiction, with terms like "digital heroin" being used to describe video games.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) Even before the 1990s, there had been a broader stance in China that video games created negative effects on those that played them, which only grew during this decade. The impact on youth was particularly of concern, as video games were known to detract students from schoolwork, leaving them unprepared to enter China's college system.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) This situation was partially created by China's [one-child policy](/wiki/One-child_policy "One-child policy"), with sibling-less children having few others to interact with and little to do outside of school.[[12]](#cite_note-npr_2007_anti-addiction-12) The anti-addiction facet also discouraged foreign companies from trying to break into the Chinese market.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4)

### Chinese console ban (2000–2015)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Chinese console ban (2000–2015)")]

The concerns about video game addiction and negative influence on the youth came to a head in June 2000. The State Council passed a bill crafted by seven ministries specifically aimed at video games. The bill established certain provisions on video game content and regulations on operations of [Internet cafés](/wiki/Internet_caf%C3%A9 "Internet café") and arcades.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4) The most significant facet of this bill was a ban on the production, import, and sale of consoles and arcade machines.[[13]](#cite_note-reuters_ban_1-13) However, the ban was poorly enforced, resulting in consoles being available on the gray market, often hacked. Importers, however, did not hide it from the government, and their stores could be found in large shopping malls. Despite this, the ban resulted in limiting of supplies and very high prices, far beyond the average citizen. The alternative was 8-bit and 16-bit clones manufactured in China in government-approved forms, such as educational computers, plug'n'plays, dvd players and handhelds.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) Global video game producers also attempted to enter the market with government approval, notably [Sega Pico](/wiki/Sega_Pico "Sega Pico") in 2002, Sony's [PlayStation 2](/wiki/PlayStation_2 "PlayStation 2") in 2004 and several of Nintendo's consoles rebranded under the [iQue](/wiki/IQue "IQue") partnership. However, with the restriction on game imports and their content, high price, huge piracy, outdated graphics, these consoles did not catch on in China.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4)[[16]](#cite_note-16) The ban did not include games available on personal computers (PC), and as a result, the PC video game market in China flourished over the next fifteen years.[[17]](#cite_note-pcgamer_china-17) Internet cafés flourished, growing from 40,000 in 2000 to over 110,000 by 2002, and have remained numerous since.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4)

The ban on arcade machines was dropped in 2009, but while arcade were permitted to operate, they had to take several safeguards to prevent excessive use by youth.[[18]](#cite_note-18) However, since such arcades offered a low-cost way to play games without a PC, they still became a thriving industry comparable to [PC gaming](/wiki/PC_game "PC game") at internet cafés. As a result, Chinese [gamers](/wiki/Gamer "Gamer") frequently visit the arcades to play [action games](/wiki/Action_game "Action game"), particularly [fighting games](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game"), and occasionally unlicensed arcade ports of popular PC or [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") such as *[Angry Birds](/wiki/Angry_Birds "Angry Birds")* or *[Plants vs. Zombies](/wiki/Plants_vs._Zombies "Plants vs. Zombies")*.[[19]](#cite_note-kotaku_china-19)

### Online gaming (2004–2008)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Online gaming (2004–2008)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Uncharted_Waters_Online_booth_at_ChinaJoy_20060728.jpg/250px-Uncharted_Waters_Online_booth_at_ChinaJoy_20060728.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uncharted_Waters_Online_booth_at_ChinaJoy_20060728.jpg)

2006 [ChinaJoy](/wiki/ChinaJoy "ChinaJoy")

Legitimate acquisition of games and the hardware to play them was still relatively expensive in China, which continued to fuel the video game clone market in China.[[20]](#cite_note-pcgamer_china_2018-20) A large number of PC gamers in China acquired software through illegal downloads and pirated software websites to avoid the cost. Developers of legitimate games in China recognized that, to compete with this black market, they had to develop games that had a free or low upfront cost model but offered a way to monetize their games over time. Many Chinese-developed games became online games offering numerous [microtransactions](/wiki/Microtransaction "Microtransaction") to recoup costs; such games could be offered at Internet cafés, which became a popular option for Chinese players that could not afford computer hardware, even as the price of computing equipment dropped over the next decade. This created a boom of [massively multiplayer online games](/wiki/Massively_multiplayer_online_game "Massively multiplayer online game") (MMOs) in the Chinese market and which helped to establish market dominance of companies like [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent"), [Perfect World](/wiki/Perfect_World_(company) "Perfect World (company)"), and [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase").[[20]](#cite_note-pcgamer_china_2018-20) PC cafés proliferated in urban centers as China's population continued to grow.[[17]](#cite_note-pcgamer_china-17) Western free-to-play and subscription-based games like *[Dota 2](/wiki/Dota_2 "Dota 2")* and *[World of Warcraft](/wiki/World_of_Warcraft "World of Warcraft")*, poised to take advantage of this model, also became successful.[[17]](#cite_note-pcgamer_china-17) It also prompted Chinese developers to develop numerous [clones](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") of popular Western games that they would offer at low cost, an issue that still persists presently.[*[timeframe?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Relative_time_references "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*][[17]](#cite_note-pcgamer_china-17)

Online gaming became of serious concern to the government around 2007, re-raising the issues of gaming addiction that had prompted the 2000 console ban. A government report claimed that 6% of teenage internet users, amounting to 3.5 million young people, were playing online games more than 40 hours a week.[[12]](#cite_note-npr_2007_anti-addiction-12) In July 2007, the government required that online game publishers and operates incorporate anti-addiction software on their games, specifically by monitoring how long underaged persons played. If a minor played for more than three hours straight, the game was to wipe half of any in-game currency that had earned that session, and lose all credits if played for more than five hours.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Additionally, these systems were required to have the player to log in using their national identification. However, at the time of implementation, not all publishers incorporated the required controls, and for those that did, players would find ways around the limitations, such as using family member IDs, or otherwise would simply play past the time requirements as there was nothing else to do beyond the video game.[[12]](#cite_note-npr_2007_anti-addiction-12)[[22]](#cite_note-cais_anti-addiction-22)

### Social and mobile gaming (2008–2014)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Social and mobile gaming (2008–2014)")]

Millions of PlayersYear010020030040050060020082010201220142016Video Game Players in China between 2008 and...

Revenue in Billion Yuan (¥)Year02040608010020082010201220142016Video Game Revenue in China between 2008 and...

Number of players (top) and video game industry revenue in China (bottom) from 2008 to 2017, showcasing the expansive growth of the industry in this period[[23]](#cite_note-23)

By 2007, the size of the Chinese video game market was estimated to be about US$1.7 billion with around 42 million players, having grown 60% from the previous year mostly driven by online gaming.[[24]](#cite_note-vb_2007_market-24)[[25]](#cite_note-auto2-25) At this stage, China's impact on the larger global market, valued at US$41.9 billion, was not considered significant, as much of it was still driven by the grey market for clones and pirated games.[[24]](#cite_note-vb_2007_market-24)[[26]](#cite_note-26) However, the rapid growth led to forecasts that China would be a major contributor to the global market within five-years time.[[24]](#cite_note-vb_2007_market-24)

Online gaming readily led way to the rise of [social network games](/wiki/Social-network_game "Social-network game") in China around 2007–2008, given that players were accustomed to free-to-play nature of online gaming. The Chinese game *[Happy Farm](/wiki/Happy_Farm "Happy Farm")* (2008) was included in *[Wired](/wiki/Wired_(magazine) "Wired (magazine)")*'s list of "The 15 Most Influential Games of the Decade" at #14, for its major influence on global social network games, particularly for having "inspired a dozen [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") clones", the largest being [Zynga](/wiki/Zynga "Zynga")'s *[FarmVille](/wiki/FarmVille "FarmVille")*.[[27]](#cite_note-27) A number of other games have since used similar game mechanics, such as *Sunshine Farm*, *Happy Farmer*, *Happy Fishpond*, *Happy Pig Farm*,[[28]](#cite_note-techgearx1-28)[[29]](#cite_note-venturebeat-29) *Farm Town*, *Country Story*, *Barn Buddy*, *Sunshine Ranch*, and *Happy Harvest*, as well as parodies such as *Jungle Extreme* and *Farm Villain*.[[30]](#cite_note-wired_farm-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

This further prepared the China market for [mobile games](/wiki/Mobile_game "Mobile game") around 2012, where there are about one billion mobile phone subscriptions, according to a [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") report,[[32]](#cite_note-32) and after [Apple](/wiki/Apple,_Inc. "Apple, Inc.") secured deals to distribute their [iPhones](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") within China.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Mobile devices in China are less expensive than computer or console hardware, and also provide Internet functionality; for many, they are the only form of Internet connectivity they have, making them popular gaming devices.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Mobile games in China grew rapidly over the next several years, growing from about 10% of the Chinese video game market in 2012 to 41% in 2016.[[35]](#cite_note-35) This expanded to more than 50% by 2018.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Furthering the growth of the social and mobile game markets was the fact that the anti-addiction measures applied to online games did not apply to these types of titles; it was not until 2017 where renewed concerns about mobile titles like *[Honor of Kings](/wiki/Honor_of_Kings "Honor of Kings")* led Tencent to implement a similar anti-addiction system for its portfolio.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

Social and mobile gaming significantly grew the Chinese video game market beyond earlier estimates. By 2013, the Chinese market for video games saw nearly a ten-fold growth since 2007, valued at US$13.5 billion of the global US$83 billion,[[38]](#cite_note-38) with over 490 million players, counting only those on personal computers; since consoles were still banned, these numbers do not take console players into account.[[4]](#cite_note-china_history-4)

### Lifting of the console ban (2014–2017)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Lifting of the console ban (2014–2017)")]

In 2014, China partially eased the restrictions on video game hardware by allowing game consoles to be manufactured in the [Shanghai Free-Trade Zone](/wiki/Shanghai_Free-Trade_Zone "Shanghai Free-Trade Zone") (FTZ) and sold in the rest of China subject to cultural inspections.[[13]](#cite_note-reuters_ban_1-13) In July 2015, the ban on video game consoles within the country was completely lifted. According to a statement from the country's Ministry of Culture, companies like Sony, Nintendo, and Microsoft – among others – were now allowed to manufacture and sell video game consoles anywhere in the country.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Microsoft and Sony quickly took advantage of the lifting of the ban, announcing sales of the Xbox One and PlayStation 4 platforms within the FTZ shortly after the 2014 announcement. Microsoft established a partnership with BesTV New Media Co, a subsidiary of the [Shanghai Media Group](/wiki/Shanghai_Media_Group "Shanghai Media Group"), to sell Xbox One units in China,[[40]](#cite_note-40) with units first shipping by September 2014.[[41]](#cite_note-ChinaRelease-41) Sony worked with [Shanghai Oriental Pearl Media](/wiki/Shanghai_Oriental_Pearl_Media "Shanghai Oriental Pearl Media") in May 2014 to establish manufacturing in the FTZ,[[42]](#cite_note-bbc_sony_ftz-42) with the PlayStation 4 and PlayStation Vita shipping into China by March 2015.[[43]](#cite_note-43) CEO of Sony Computer Entertainment [Andrew House](/wiki/Andrew_House "Andrew House") explained in September 2013 that the company intended to use the PlayStation Vita TV as a low-cost alternative for consumers in an attempt to penetrate the Chinese video game market.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Both Microsoft and Sony have identified China as a key market for their next generation of consoles, the [Xbox Series X](/wiki/Xbox_Series_X "Xbox Series X") and [PlayStation 5](/wiki/PlayStation_5 "PlayStation 5") respectively.[[45]](#cite_note-vb_2018_market-45)

Nintendo did not initially seek to bring the [Wii U](/wiki/Wii_U "Wii U") into China; Nintendo of America president [Reggie Fils-Aimé](/wiki/Reggie_Fils-Aim%C3%A9 "Reggie Fils-Aimé") stated that China was of interest to the company after the ban was lifted, but considered that there were similar difficulties with establishing sales there as they had recently had with Brazil.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Later, Nintendo had teamed up with [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent") by April 2019 to help sell and distribute the Nintendo Switch as well as aid its games through the Chinese government approval process led by [National Radio and Television Administration](/wiki/National_Radio_and_Television_Administration "National Radio and Television Administration").[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The [Nintendo Switch](/wiki/Nintendo_Switch "Nintendo Switch") went on sale in China on December 10, 2019, though unlike the international version; this unit included several concessions to region-lock it to China.[[49]](#cite_note-49) Even with the ban lifted, console sales were slow, as consoles require dedicated space in home and did not have additional functionality, like personal computers, and further slowed by continued popularity of Internet cafés. The hardware grey market also persisted, drawing away legitimate sales of consoles.[[50]](#cite_note-uscc-50) Of the US$37.9 billion industry revenue in 2018,[[51]](#cite_note-51) only about $1 billion was attributed to console sales. It is expected that as interest in legitimate sales of consoles increases in the future, the grey market will wane.[[45]](#cite_note-vb_2018_market-45) Despite official availability of the Switch, imported and grey-market sales of Switch consoles still dominated China; while Nintendo and Tencent reported that a million Switch consoles had been sold by January 2021, the total number of Switch consoles in use within the country was estimated to be at least twice as high due to imported, non-region-locked versions.[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Approvals freeze and further steps to restrict youth gaming (2018–2023)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Approvals freeze and further steps to restrict youth gaming (2018–2023)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/%E5%B4%A9%E5%9D%8F%E6%98%9F%E7%A9%B9%E9%93%81%E9%81%93_%E5%9B%9B%E5%B7%9D%E6%88%90%E9%83%BD.jpg/250px-%E5%B4%A9%E5%9D%8F%E6%98%9F%E7%A9%B9%E9%93%81%E9%81%93_%E5%9B%9B%E5%B7%9D%E6%88%90%E9%83%BD.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E5%B4%A9%E5%9D%8F%E6%98%9F%E7%A9%B9%E9%93%81%E9%81%93_%E5%9B%9B%E5%B7%9D%E6%88%90%E9%83%BD.jpg)

The LED advertising hoardings of *[Honkai: Star Rail](/wiki/Honkai:_Star_Rail "Honkai: Star Rail")* in [Chengdu](/wiki/Chengdu "Chengdu"), [Sichuan](/wiki/Sichuan "Sichuan")

In March 2018, after the structural re-organization of State Administration of Radio and Television (SART), over a period of several months, no new game licenses were given out. Further, MOC had made the process of getting these licenses more stringent.[[53]](#cite_note-bloomberg_aug2018-53) This period has significantly impacted Tencent, one of the largest publishers of video games for China. In August 2018, Tencent was forced to pull from sale their version of *[Monster Hunter World](/wiki/Monster_Hunter_World "Monster Hunter World")* from China as they had not got their license for it and the government received complaints about its content. Tencent were also blocked from publishing personal computer versions of *[PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds](/wiki/PlayerUnknown%27s_Battlegrounds "PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds")* and *[Fortnite Battle Royale](/wiki/Fortnite_Battle_Royale "Fortnite Battle Royale")*. The license freezes was reported to have significant effects on those game publishers and developers that rely on Chinese sales.[[53]](#cite_note-bloomberg_aug2018-53) In late August 2018, the [Chinese Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China") called on the Chinese government and SART to also address the growing issue of [myopia](/wiki/Myopia "Myopia") in children which was attributed to long hours of gaming on small screens like with mobile devices. The Ministry of Education had asked SART to consider placing restrictions on the number of hours each young player can play a game. On news of this, Tencent shares lost 5% of their value, an estimated US$20 billion on the stock market, the next day.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) A further approval route was closed by Chinese authorities in October 2018; this "green channel" route, which had been in place by August 2018, allowed a game to have a period of one month on the market for purposes of consumer testing without having full government approval, but had been seen by game publishers as temporary relief from the current ban. Tencent had been planning on distributing and monetizing from *[Fortnite Battle Royale](/wiki/Fortnite_Battle_Royale "Fortnite Battle Royale")* via this method before this route was closed.[[56]](#cite_note-bloomberg_oct2018-56)

With China's effective ban of new games continuing into October 2018, Chinese players have found other routes of getting new games, which include using [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") which uses overseas servers.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Further, existing titles released before the freeze that continue to offer new content have seen a resurgence in players and spending as a result.[[56]](#cite_note-bloomberg_oct2018-56) To comply with the planned new rules, Tencent announced that all mobile games it manages in China will require users to use their Chinese ID to play. This will be used by Tencent to track the time that minors play the game and implement time limitations on them, among other steps to meet new regulations.[[58]](#cite_note-58)

By December 2018, the Chinese government had formed the [Online Game Ethics Committee](/wiki/Online_Game_Ethics_Committee "Online Game Ethics Committee") falling under the [National Radio and Television Administration](/wiki/National_Radio_and_Television_Administration "National Radio and Television Administration"), which will review all games to be published in China for appropriate content as well as issues related to childhood myopia.[[59]](#cite_note-59) The committee, by the end of the year, had restarted the approval process and will be working through a backlog of submissions to review in an expedited manner to allow new games to be released.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Initial approvals to 80 back-logged titles was granted within days, but notably lacked games published by Tencent and Netease, the two largest publishers in China.[[61]](#cite_note-61) After several more rounds, Tencent had two games approved near the end of January 2019, but did not include either *[Fortnite Battle Royale](/wiki/Fortnite_Battle_Royale "Fortnite Battle Royale")* or *[PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds](/wiki/PlayerUnknown%27s_Battlegrounds "PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds")*, two major titles that were financial drivers in other countries.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

A second freeze on approvals started in February 2019, as any further approvals on new games were suspending until the committee has been able to clear the backlog of the titles from the prior freeze. By this point, only about 350 games had been approved from the previous freeze.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

According to China's State Administration of Press and Publication, the freezes were put in place as the video game industry had grown too rapidly in China at a rate that passed the capabilities for regulation to keep up. The second freeze that started in February 2019 was to put in place to give regulators a change to tune the game approval process to meet the current market size. The freeze is expected to be lifted in April 2019, alongside a new set of regulations for game approvals. These new changes include limiting the number of games that can be approved each year to around 5,000 games, strictly banning [video game clones](/wiki/Video_game_clone "Video game clone") and games with obscene content, and placing more anti-addiction controls on mobile titles aimed at younger players.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The nearly year-long freeze has had rippling effects on the global video game industry. Whereas in 2017, around 9,600 new games were approved, only around 1,980 were approved within 2018.[[65]](#cite_note-65) Tencent had been one of the top 10 companies in the world at the start of 2018, but by October, its stock had dropped in value by 40%, an estimated US$230 billion, and knocked the company out of the top ten.[[66]](#cite_note-66) [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") attributed revenue loss in the fourth quarter of 2018 to China's approval freeze, which had also affected mobile video game apps.[[67]](#cite_note-67) The freeze was expected to impact total revenues of the video game industry in 2019, with one analysis projecting a decline in revenue from the previous year, the first time in only a decade.[[68]](#cite_note-vgmarket-68)

The Chinese government continued to push on restrictions on gaming after the approvals freeze was lifted, asserting its efforts were to restrict the influence of gaming on youth. The government has placed restrictions on the amount of time minors can play video games, first in 2019 to 90 minutes per day on weekdays and three hours on weekends,[[69]](#cite_note-nytimes_2019_ban-69) and then to only one hour per day on weekends by 2021.[[70]](#cite_note-wsj_2021_ban-70) The government has required that all online games to implement strong authentication protocols developed by the government as to track players' time in a game as well.[[71]](#cite_note-scmp_2020_protocols-71) Additionally, the government banned minors under the age of 16 from registering for [livestreaming](/wiki/Livestreaming "Livestreaming") services.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

Since March 2021, there had been new pressure on video games, kicked off by statements made by [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") during the annual [Two Sessions](/wiki/Two_Sessions "Two Sessions") meetings where he claimed that video games could have a bad influence on the minds of children who are psychologically immature.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The government had stopped approval of games starting in August 2021 in an apparent new lockdown related to game content.[[74]](#cite_note-scmp_new_rules_sept21-74) The continued pressure by the government on the Chinese game sector started to take an effect on the economic valuation of the largest companies. An article published by the state-owned newspaper *[Economic Information Daily](/wiki/Economic_Information_Daily "Economic Information Daily")* published a report in August 2021 that initially stated that online video games were an "opium for the mind", that gaming addiction was on the rise, and that there should be stronger government regulations. While the article was pulled and later republished without the "opium" statement, its effects caused shares of Tencent to drop by 10% initially that day in trading, though had recovered some after the revised article was published. Similar drops were seen with NetEase and Bilibili.[[75]](#cite_note-75) Later in September 2021, when both Tencent and Netease were notified by the government of an upcoming hearing and reminding them than violations of their youth gaming restrictions would be seriously dealt with, both companies' stocks dropped by about 10% due to fears that the government may be clamping down more on gaming in the future, including another potential approvals freeze.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Over 200 Chinese game companies, including Tencent and NetEase, signed a statement that month pledging that they will work to regulate youth gaming under the government's new regulations, as well as to enforce new rules relating to games involved "effeminate" portrayal of men in games.[[77]](#cite_note-77) As reported by the *South China Morning Post*, an internal memo sent by the state's gaming trade organization to game companies in September 2021 for purposes of training further clarified that that government saw video games not as "pure entertainment" but as a form of art and thus works that must uphold "a correct set of values" related to China's heritage and culture, and would be more restrictive in what games they would approve within the country.[[74]](#cite_note-scmp_new_rules_sept21-74) The memo described games that have "blurred moral boundaries", where the player has an option of being good or bad within the game, and suggested that such games may need instead to restrict players to a specific moral path. Further, the memo identified that games that gave a "revisionist" form of history, or appeared more Japanese than Chinese, would likely fail to be approved.[[74]](#cite_note-scmp_new_rules_sept21-74)

According to the *South China Morning Post*, the approvals for new games persisted through the end of 2021, and due to the lack of approvals, more than 14,000 game-related companies were deregistered in China through 2021.[[78]](#cite_note-78) The approvals freeze was lifted by April 2022 when new approvals were announced.[[79]](#cite_note-79) In December 2021, players within China reported that the international version of Steam had been blocked in China. However, it turned out to be a partial disruption of Steam's web version and as of 2024, the International version of Steam is still fully accessible in China barring some occasional connection issues [[80]](#cite_note-80) The government also banned the live streaming of unapproved games in April 2022.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

A report issued in November 2022 by research firm CNG and the China Game Industry Group Committee, both with strong associations with the Chinese regulatory bodies, stated that the steps taken to reduce youth video game addiction had been working, with more than 70% of the country's youth gaming less than three hours a week. The report suggested that the regulatory bodies should be able to back off on the tight restrictions they had placed on publishers like Tencent and NetEase over the prior few years.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

The NPPA issued new proposed rules in December 2023 aimed to further reduce the amount of time citizens play games and reduce in-game spending. These rules would prevent games from having daily or first-time login rewards and limit how much virtual currency that players could keep within in-game wallets. These rules appeared to be directly aimed at MMOs and [gacha games](/wiki/Gacha_game "Gacha game"), both popular titles within China, and as a result, both Tencent and NetEase saw major stock losses at the announcement of these rules totalling $80 billion.[[83]](#cite_note-83)[[84]](#cite_note-84) The government quickly walked back on these recommendations, and reported let go of the official that had drawn them by January 2024.[[85]](#cite_note-85)

### Global growth (2024–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Global growth (2024–present)")]

As of 2024, China has begun approving more and more games as it seeks to revive the industry after a prolonged down period after a 2021 crackdown. The government has approved an average of 107 games per month in 2024. This came along with China's first AAA game *[Black Myth: Wukong](/wiki/Black_Myth:_Wukong "Black Myth: Wukong")*. Some analysts expect *Black Myth*'s success to have a positive impact on the Chinese gaming industry as the government could now be more inclined to issue game licenses to encourage domestic studios to work on more AAA games considering *Black Myth: Wukong*'s domestic and international success [[86]](#cite_note-86)

China only represented 0.8% of total Steam users in 2014 and reached 23.6% in March 2025, a figure placing the country above the United States in terms of users, indicating an increasing relevance of Chinese audiences as video game consumers on the global stage.[[87]](#cite_note-87)

## Online gaming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Online gaming")]

Online gaming in China represents one of the largest and fastest growing [Internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet") business sectors in the world. As of 2022, China is the second largest market for online games after the United States.[[88]](#cite_note-88) In 2023, the country has 668 million internet users playing online games and the industry was worth US$42 billion.[[89]](#cite_note-89) 53.8% of gamers are male, 46.2% are female.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Online games in China fall into two primary categories: [MMORPGs](/wiki/Massively_Multiplayer_Online_Roleplaying_Game "Massively Multiplayer Online Roleplaying Game") and [MOCGs](/wiki/Multiplayer_Online_Casual_Game "Multiplayer Online Casual Game"). The former have a predilection for persistent online worlds where hundreds to thousands of game players can interact simultaneously; the latter is a generic term for games played competitively online without the existence of a persistent online realm (games as simple as online [Ma Jiang](/wiki/Mahjong "Mahjong") and online competitive [card games](/wiki/Card_game "Card game") would fall under this category). In 2011, there were over 100 million Chinese MMO gamers.

Official Chinese statistics regarding online gaming state that as of the close of 2006 revenue from China's online gaming industry reached RMB 8 billion or around US$1.04 billion, with earnings reaching around RMB 33 billion or US$4.3 billion. Additionally, while [Japanese](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [American](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and [South Korean](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") companies have traditionally dominated the market, Chinese developed software now holds a 65% market share on the mainland, with an additional 20 million in revenue generated by users outside of China.[[91]](#cite_note-auto-91)

### Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Games")]

[QQ Games](/wiki/QQ_Games "QQ Games") is one such popular online client. Growth was driven in part by China's most popular online game, [Netease](/wiki/Netease "Netease")'s *[Fantasy Westward Journey](/wiki/Fantasy_Westward_Journey "Fantasy Westward Journey")*, which now has 1.66 million peak [concurrent users](/wiki/Concurrent_user "Concurrent user"). Another contributor is Giant's *[Zhengtu Online](/wiki/Zhengtu_Online "Zhengtu Online")*, which has 1.52 million peak concurrent users.[[25]](#cite_note-auto2-25)

## Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Industry")]

### Publishers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Video_games_in_China&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Publishers")]

Today, the video game market is dominated by the Tencent Games division of [Tencent Holdings](/wiki/Tencent_Holdings "Tencent Holdings"), which is estimated to contribute to 46% of the overall revenue in China, and nearly 10% of the global video game market as of 2017, making it the largest video game company in the world.[[50]](#cite_note-uscc-50) [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase"), which contributes to around 15% of overall revenue in China, is the second largest video game company in China, as well as the seventh largest in the world as of 2017.[[50]](#cite_note-uscc-50)[[92]](#cite_note-92) Other major players include [Perfect World](/wiki/Perfect_World_(company) "Perfect World (company)"), Shunrong, and [Shanda](/wiki/Shanda "Shanda").[[50]](#cite_note-uscc-50)

These companies are noted for having made aggressive investments in foreign video game developers, particularly from South Korea and the United States, and for making strategic agreements with other entities to serve as the China-based operating arm for foreign interests to meet Chinese government regulations.[[50]](#cite_note-uscc-50) Notably, Tencent's acquisitions have included: US$400 million to acquire [Riot Games](/wiki/Riot_Games "Riot Games") in 2011 to gain right to the online game *[League of Legends](/wiki/League_of_Legends "League of Legends")* and US$8.6 billion for [Supercell](/wiki/Supercell_(video_game_company) "Supercell (video game company)") in 2016 for its mobile game *[Clash of Clans](/wiki/Clash_of_Clans "Clash of Clans")*.[[50]](#cite_note-uscc-50) Among major investments include approximately 5% of [Activision](/wiki/Activision "Activision") in 2013,[[93]](#cite_note-93) a 40% interest in [Epic Games](/wiki/Epic_Games "Epic Games") in 2013,[[94]](#cite_note-tencent-94) and a 5% interest in [Ubisoft](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft") in 2018.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

The 10 largest online game companies by revenue in 2017 are:[[96]](#cite_note-auto1-96)

1. [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent"): Tencent Games is the Interactive Entertainment Division (aka IED) of Tencent.
2. [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase"): a popular online portal in China, also branched out in the space of MMORPGs with the release of Westward Journey. The game, based on ancient westward travels on the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") (a popular theme from Chinese developed MMORPGs), has gone through two iterations; it was re-released as Westward Journey II due to numerous problems with the initial release, and its game engine was used to develop [Fantasy Westward Journey](/wiki/Fantasy_Westward_Journey "Fantasy Westward Journey"), which is currently the most popular MMORPG in China (based on PCU numbers).[[97]](#cite_note-97)
3. [YY](/wiki/YY.com "YY.com")
4. [37 Interactive](/wiki/37Games "37Games")
5. [Perfect World](/wiki/Perfect_World_(company) "Perfect World (company)")
6. Elex
7. [IGG](/wiki/IGG_Inc. "IGG Inc.")
8. [Alpha Group](/wiki/Alpha_Group_Co. "Alpha Group Co.")
9. Century Huatong Group (owner of [Shanda](/wiki/Shanda "Shanda")). [Shanda](/wiki/Shanda "Shanda") produces and supports many popular MMORPGs. The company is significant because it introduced a new [online payment](/wiki/Online_payment "Online payment") system with the release of [Legend of Mir 2](/wiki/Legend_of_Mir_2 "Legend of Mir 2") in 2001. Instead of charging users for the initial purchase of the game, Shanda gave the software away free-of-charge and decided to charge users for time spent playing in game. This payment system specifically counteracted piracy because the company could maintain easier control over the time users spent in the game, rather than attempt to limit the game's distribution.[[98]](#cite_note-98) Shanda maintains a large number of MMORPGs in China developed by Western, Korean and native Chinese companies; the latter two regions produce Shanda's most popular games. The company also maintains numerous casual games as well, with platforms supporting [chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") and other non-persistent world games.[[99]](#cite_note-99)
10. Kunlun Tech

[The9](/wiki/The9 "The9") (第九城市) is similar to Shanda Entertainment, it specifically maintains and produces MMORPG content for the Chinese gamer base. The9 is notable because of its partnership with [Blizzard Entertainment](/wiki/Blizzard_Entertainment "Blizzard Entertainment") in bringing [World of Warcraft](/wiki/World_of_Warcraft "World of Warcraft") (the most popular MMORPG outside of Asia) to China. World of Warcraft is the most popular western MMORPG in Asia, and one of the most popular in China in general. Recent statistics place its peak concurrent users at around 688,000, easily among the top MMORPGs in the country.[[100]](#cite_note-100) The9 also implemented a pay-for-time system for the game, which differs from the monthly subscription payment structure used by Blizzard in other territories. In April 2009, World of Warcraft owner Activision Blizzard announced it had selected The9 competitor NetEase to operate the game in China. The9's license expired on June 7, 2009.[[101]](#cite_note-101)

In 2023, China's [gaming industry](/wiki/Gaming_industry "Gaming industry") continued to show strong competitiveness and resilience in the global market. Compared to 2017, the ranking and revenue structure in 2023 has changed significantly, with headline companies such as [Tencent](/wiki/Tencent "Tencent") and [NetEase](/wiki/NetEase "NetEase") still dominating, but emerging companies such as [miHoYo](/wiki/MiHoYo "MiHoYo"), [HYPERGRYPH](/wiki/Arknights "Arknights") and [Game Science](/wiki/Game_Science "Game Science") are rapidly emerging as industry highlights with high-quality titles such as *[Genshin Impact](/wiki/Genshin_Impact "Genshin Impact")*, *[Arknights](/wiki/Arknights "Arknights")*, and *[Black Myth: Wukong](/wiki/Black_Myth:_Wukong "Black Myth: Wukong")*. In addition, game genres have become more diversified, from [MOBAs](/wiki/Multiplayer_online_battle_arena "Multiplayer online battle arena") and [shooters](/wiki/Shooter_game "Shooter game") to [tactical role-playing](/wiki/Tactical_role-playing_game "Tactical role-playing game") and female-oriented [card games](/wiki/Card_game "Card game"), further segmenting market demand. These changes reflect the progress made by China's game industry in terms of technological innovation, content creation and global market adaptation, and also reveal the industry's development trends and opportunities in the new market environment.
